text:
I thoroughly loved the essay and I recommend everyone to read it. And here I want to add one other type of person who easily, even if a little bit guiltily, goes Nazi.

It's the slightly insecure type who rarely attends parties, generally likes people and helps many of them, and is probably a bit judgemental towards certain sorts of personalities that don't fit nicely in their worldview.

Those slightly insecure people are easily conditioned by propaganda, and their worldview is easily swayed. It's simple to tell them what particular kinds of guys they should dislike. And while they won't hurt anyone directly nor will they cheer for lynchings, they will make excuses for why "there is no other way if we want to save our society."

Those are probably not the most despicable types but very likely the most numerous.

text:
There is another angle to this I often encounter with people who believe that ancient civilizations built things better than we do now. They say, "Look, the pyramids have been there for millennia, while our bridges will be undone so quickly if not maintained."

Yes, but the pyramids are stupidly simple compared to a suspension bridge. A pyramid is basically just a very heavy pile of rocks. It is not used for anything, it is mostly not hollow inside, its stress profile is almost constant compared to that of a bridge.

text:
Disposable software will very quickly lead us to the state of the world where we summon powerful demons to do our bidding left and right. The necessity to understand things in order to make them happen will go away, and that happens already.

Fewer and fewer people will be able to write code themselves, more of them will only prompt machines to make things for them. There will be a shortage of good software engineers but that will be fine for quite a long time because the machines, when being prompted correctly, will output something working almost all the time.

The quality of machine software will be lower but it will be so cheap for so long that people will get used to bugs and errors, just as we've gotten used to those over the last decade as software has grown noticeably buggier.

At some point we may recover from that pit if non-software facets of the world are in an okay state. If there are spare resources, we can spend some on training a new generation of good software engineers who will fix the underlying infrastructure. Or maybe things won't be going that well, and we will notice too late that it's getting irreparable, and then the civilization will crumble, slowly at first and then faster and faster into a new dark age.

Thank you for coming to my blackpill TED talk.
